tracks.name: Python — First Steps
tracks.description: Start from zero. Print values, store data, do math, and shape strings.

lessons.title: 1. Hello, print
lessons.summary: Use `print()` to send text to standard output.

teaching.text: Programs talk to you by printing. In Python, the built-in printer is `print()`.

print('Hi there')
# → Hi there
Lines that start with `#` are comments. Python ignores them.

lessons.task: Make the program print exactly: Hello, World!
lessons.starterCode: # Use print to say Hello, World!
hints: Call `print()` with a string in quotes.

lessons.title: 2. Print more than one line
lessons.summary: Each `print()` call writes a new line by default.

teaching.text: Use separate `print()` calls when each value should appear on its own line.

print('Ready')
print('Go')
# → Ready
# → Go
Python runs these statements from top to bottom.

lessons.task: Print `Learn` on the first line and `Python` on the second line.
lessons.starterCode: # Print two lines: Learn, then Python
hints: Put `Learn` in the first call and `Python` in the second.

lessons.title: 3. Text is a string
lessons.summary: Text wrapped in quotes is a Python string.

teaching.text: Python treats quoted text as data. Single and double quotes both create strings.

print("Python")
print('rocks')
# → Python
# → rocks
The opening and closing quote marks must match.

lessons.task: Print the string `Code is data`.
lessons.starterCode: # Print the text Code is data
hints: Wrap the text in matching quotes.

lessons.title: 4. Store a value
lessons.summary: A variable gives a value a reusable name.

teaching.text: Use `=` to assign a value to a variable, then use the variable name to read it.

language = 'Python'
print(language)
# → Python
Do not put quotes around a variable name when you want its stored value.

lessons.task: Create a variable named `tool` with the value `seec0de`, then print it.
lessons.starterCode: # Store seec0de in a variable named tool, then print it

lessons.title: 5. Update a variable
lessons.summary: Assigning a new value replaces the value stored in a variable.

teaching.text: Variables can change while a program runs. Reassign the same name to replace its value.

score = 2
score = 5
print(score)
# → 5
Only the latest assigned value is stored.

lessons.task: Set `level` to `1`, update it to `2`, then print `level`.
lessons.starterCode: # Set level to 1, change it to 2, then print it
hints: Print the variable after the second assignment.

lessons.title: 6. Add and subtract
lessons.summary: Python uses arithmetic operators to calculate numeric values.

teaching.text: Use `+` for addition and `-` for subtraction. Python calculates the expression before printing it.
Numbers are not wrapped in quotes.

lessons.task: Calculate `18 - 7` and print the result.
lessons.starterCode: # Print the result of 18 minus 7

lessons.title: 7. Multiply and divide
lessons.summary: Use `*` to multiply and `/` to divide.

teaching.text: Multiplication uses `*`. Regular division uses `/` and produces a decimal number.
Even when division is exact, `/` returns a floating-point value.

lessons.task: Multiply `6` by `7` and print the result.
lessons.starterCode: # Print 6 multiplied by 7

lessons.title: 8. Join strings
lessons.summary: The `+` operator joins strings into one string.

teaching.text: When both values are strings, `+` concatenates them instead of doing arithmetic.

first = 'sea'
second = 'code'
print(first + second)
# → seacode
Add a space inside one string when the output needs a space.

lessons.task: Join `Hello, ` and `Python!` with `+`, then print the result.
lessons.starterCode: # Join the two strings with + and print them
hints: Store or use both strings with their exact punctuation.
The first string should include the space after the comma.

lessons.title: 9. Put values inside text
lessons.summary: An f-string inserts variable values inside a string.

teaching.text: Prefix a string with `f` and place variable names inside `{}` to embed their values.

name = 'Ada'
print(f'Hello, {name}')
# → Hello, Ada
The braces contain Python expressions, not quoted text.

lessons.task: Store `3` in `lessons` and use an f-string to print `Lessons complete: 3`.
lessons.starterCode: # Store 3 in lessons, then print the message with an f-string
hints: Use `{lessons}` inside a string prefixed with `f`.

lessons.title: 10. Measure and transform text
lessons.summary: Built-ins and string methods can inspect or transform text.

teaching.text: `len()` counts characters. Methods such as `.upper()` return a transformed string.

word = 'code'
print(len(word))
print(word.upper())
# → 4
# → CODE
The original string stays unchanged unless you assign the transformed value.

lessons.task: Store `python` in `word`, then print its length and its uppercase form on separate lines.
lessons.starterCode:
# Store python in word
# Print its length, then print it in uppercase

tracks.name: Python — Control Flow
tracks.description: Make decisions and repeat work with booleans, conditions, and loops.

lessons.title: 11. True or False
lessons.summary: A boolean value is either `True` or `False`.

teaching.text: Booleans represent yes-or-no states. Python spells them `True` and `False` with capital first letters.

is_ready = True
print(is_ready)
# → True
`True` and `False` are values, so do not wrap them in quotes.

lessons.task: Create `is_learning` with the boolean value `True`, then print it.
lessons.starterCode: # Store True in is_learning, then print it

lessons.title: 12. Compare values
lessons.summary: Comparisons produce boolean results.

teaching.text: Use operators such as `==`, `!=`, `<`, and `>` to compare values.

print(5 > 3)
print(5 == 3)
# → True
# → False
`=` assigns a value; `==` checks whether two values are equal.

lessons.task: Check whether `10` is greater than `7` and print the result.
lessons.starterCode: # Print the result of comparing 10 and 7

lessons.title: 13. Run code conditionally
lessons.summary: An `if` block runs only when its condition is true.

teaching.text: Put a boolean expression after `if`, end the line with `:`, and indent the code that depends on it.

score = 8
if score > 5:
    print('Passed')
# → Passed
Indentation defines which statements belong to the `if` block.

lessons.task: Set `temperature` to `30`. If it is greater than `25`, print `Hot`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Set temperature to 30
# If it is above 25, print Hot

lessons.title: 14. Choose between two paths
lessons.summary: An `else` block runs when the `if` condition is false.

teaching.text: Use `if` for one path and `else` for the fallback path.
Exactly one of these two branches runs.

lessons.task: Set `number` to `9`. Print `Even` if it is divisible by `2`; otherwise print `Odd`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Set number to 9
# Print Even or Odd with if/else

lessons.title: 15. Check several conditions
lessons.summary: Use `elif` to test another condition when earlier ones fail.

teaching.text: An `if`/`elif`/`else` chain checks conditions from top to bottom and runs the first matching branch.
Order matters: put stricter or higher thresholds first.

lessons.task: Set `signal` to `yellow`. Print `Go` for `green`, `Wait` for `yellow`, and `Stop` otherwise.
lessons.starterCode:
# Set signal to yellow
# Use if, elif, and else to print the correct action
hints: Use `elif signal == 'yellow':` for the second branch.

lessons.title: 16. Combine conditions
lessons.summary: Logical operators combine or reverse boolean expressions.

teaching.text: `and` requires both sides to be true, `or` requires at least one, and `not` reverses a boolean.

age = 20
has_ticket = True
print(age >= 18 and has_ticket)
# → True
Parentheses can make combined conditions easier to read.

lessons.task: Set `logged_in` to `True` and `is_admin` to `False`, then print whether either value is true.
lessons.starterCode:
# Create logged_in and is_admin
# Print whether either one is True
lessons.solution:
logged_in = True
is_admin = False
print(logged_in or is_admin)

lessons.title: 17. Repeat with while
lessons.summary: A `while` loop repeats while its condition remains true.

teaching.text: A `while` loop is useful when repetition depends on a changing condition.

count = 1
while count <= 3:
    print(count)
    count += 1
# → 1
# → 2
# → 3
Update the loop variable so the condition eventually becomes false.

lessons.task: Use a `while` loop to print `1`, `2`, and `3` on separate lines.
lessons.starterCode:
# Start count at 1
# Use while to print through 3

lessons.title: 18. Stop a loop early
lessons.summary: `break` exits the nearest loop immediately.

teaching.text: Use `break` when a loop should stop as soon as a condition is met.

number = 1
while True:
    print(number)
    if number == 2:
        break
    number += 1
# → 1
# → 2
`while True` runs forever unless something inside exits the loop.

lessons.task: Use `while True` to print `Searching`, then immediately stop the loop with `break`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Start an infinite loop
# Print Searching once, then break
hints: Indent both `print('Searching')` and `break` inside the loop.

lessons.title: 19. Count with for
lessons.summary: A `for` loop visits each value produced by `range()`.

teaching.text: `range(start, stop)` produces integers from `start` up to, but not including, `stop`.

for number in range(1, 4):
    print(number)
# → 1
# → 2
# → 3
The stop value is excluded.

lessons.task: Use `for` and `range()` to print `2`, `3`, and `4` on separate lines.
lessons.starterCode: # Loop over the numbers 2 through 4 and print each one
hints: Use `5` as the stop value because it is excluded.

lessons.title: 20. Loop through text
lessons.summary: A `for` loop can visit each character in a string.

teaching.text: Strings are iterable, so a `for` loop can process one character at a time.

for letter in 'cat':
    print(letter)
# → c
# → a
# → t
The loop variable receives the next character on each iteration.

lessons.task: Loop through the string `code` and print each character on its own line.
lessons.starterCode: # Loop through code and print each character

tracks.name: Python — Data Structures
tracks.description: Group, access, update, and organize values with Python's core collections.

lessons.title: 21. Store many values
lessons.summary: A list stores an ordered collection of values.

teaching.text: Create a list with square brackets and separate its items with commas.

colors = ['red', 'blue']
print(colors)
# → ['red', 'blue']
Lists can grow, shrink, and change after creation.

lessons.task: Create a list named `languages` containing `Python` and `JavaScript`, then print it.
lessons.starterCode: # Create languages with Python and JavaScript, then print it
hints: Use square brackets to create the list.
Put both strings inside the brackets, separated by a comma.

lessons.title: 22. Read a list item
lessons.summary: Use an index to access one item in a list.

teaching.text: List indexes start at `0`, so the first item is at index `0`.

tools = ['Git', 'Python', 'SQL']
print(tools[1])
# → Python
An index outside the list raises an error.

lessons.task: Create `numbers = [10, 20, 30]` and print the second item.
lessons.starterCode: # Create the numbers list and print its second item
hints: The second item is at index `1`.

lessons.title: 23. Change a list item
lessons.summary: Assign to a list index to replace an item.

teaching.text: Lists are mutable, so you can replace an item at a specific index.

status = ['todo', 'todo']
status[0] = 'done'
print(status)
# → ['done', 'todo']
Only the selected index changes.

lessons.task: Create `scores = [5, 8, 9]`, change the first score to `7`, then print the list.
lessons.starterCode: # Create scores, replace the first item with 7, then print it
hints: The first item is at index `0`.

lessons.title: 24. Add to a list
lessons.summary: The `.append()` method adds one item to the end of a list.

teaching.text: Call `.append(value)` when you need to grow a list by one item.

queue = ['A']
queue.append('B')
print(queue)
# → ['A', 'B']
`.append()` changes the existing list.

lessons.task: Create `tasks = ['build']`, append `test`, then print the list.
lessons.starterCode: # Create tasks, append test, then print the list

lessons.title: 25. Take part of a list
lessons.summary: A slice creates a new list from part of another list.

teaching.text: Use `list[start:stop]` to copy items from `start` up to, but not including, `stop`.

values = [1, 2, 3, 4]
print(values[1:3])
# → [2, 3]
The stop index is excluded.

lessons.task: Create `letters = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']` and print the slice containing `b` and `c`.
lessons.starterCode: # Create letters and print a slice containing b and c

lessons.title: 26. Loop through a list
lessons.summary: A `for` loop can process each item in a list.

teaching.text: Each loop iteration assigns the next list item to the loop variable.

names = ['Ada', 'Linus']
for name in names:
    print(name)
# → Ada
# → Linus
The loop follows the list's order.

lessons.task: Loop through `['plan', 'build', 'ship']` and print each item on its own line.
lessons.starterCode: # Create the list and print each item with a for loop
hints: Store the strings in a list.
Use `for item in steps:` and print `item`.

lessons.title: 27. Store key-value pairs
lessons.summary: A dictionary maps unique keys to values.

teaching.text: Create a dictionary with `{}` and write each entry as `key: value`.
Access a value by placing its key inside square brackets.

lessons.task: Create `profile` with the key `role` mapped to `developer`, then print that value.
lessons.starterCode: # Create profile with role set to developer, then print the role

lessons.title: 28. Add and update dictionary values
lessons.summary: Assigning to a dictionary key adds it or replaces its value.

teaching.text: Use square-bracket assignment to create a new key or update an existing one.

settings = {'theme': 'light'}
settings['theme'] = 'dark'
settings['sound'] = True
print(settings['theme'])
# → dark
Dictionary keys must be unique.

lessons.task: Create `game = {'score': 10}`, update `score` to `15`, then print it.
lessons.starterCode: # Create game, update its score to 15, then print the score
hints: Print the value stored under the `score` key.

lessons.title: 29. Store fixed data
lessons.summary: A tuple is an ordered collection that cannot be changed.

teaching.text: Create a tuple with parentheses. Read its items with indexes just like a list.

point = (4, 7)
print(point[0])
# → 4
Tuple items cannot be reassigned after creation.

lessons.task: Create `size = (1920, 1080)` and print the second value.
lessons.starterCode: # Create the size tuple and print its second value
hints: Put the two numbers inside parentheses.
The second value is at index `1`.

lessons.title: 30. Keep unique values
lessons.summary: A set stores unique values and removes duplicates.

teaching.text: Create a set with braces. Repeated values appear only once.
Sets are unordered, so do not rely on their printed item order.

lessons.task: Create a set from `1`, `1`, `2`, and `3`, then print the number of unique values.
lessons.starterCode:
# Create a set with 1, 1, 2, and 3
# Print how many unique values it contains
hints: Use braces to create the set.

tracks.name: Python — Functions
tracks.description: Package reusable behavior with definitions, parameters, return values, and scope.

lessons.title: 31. Define a function
lessons.summary: A function groups statements under a reusable name.

teaching.text: Use `def`, a function name, parentheses, and a colon. Indent the function body.

def greet():
    print('Hello')

greet()
# → Hello
Defining a function does not run it; calling it does.

lessons.task: Define a function named `launch` that prints `Launching`, then call it.
lessons.starterCode: # Define launch so it prints Launching, then call it
hints: Call the function with `launch()` after its definition.

lessons.title: 32. Pass a value in
lessons.summary: A parameter lets a function receive a value from its caller.

teaching.text: Place a parameter name inside the function's parentheses, then pass an argument when calling it.

def greet(name):
    print(f'Hello, {name}')

greet('Ada')
# → Hello, Ada
The argument is assigned to the parameter during the call.

lessons.task: Define `show_language(language)` to print `Learning: <language>`, then call it with `Python`.
lessons.starterCode: # Define show_language with one parameter, then call it with Python

lessons.title: 33. Use multiple parameters
lessons.summary: A function can receive several arguments through several parameters.

teaching.text: Separate parameter names and arguments with commas, keeping their positions aligned.

def add(a, b):
    print(a + b)

add(2, 3)
# → 5
By default, arguments are matched to parameters by position.

lessons.task: Define `multiply(a, b)` to print their product, then call it with `6` and `7`.
lessons.starterCode: # Define multiply with two parameters, then call it with 6 and 7
hints: Put `a` and `b` inside the definition's parentheses.

lessons.title: 34. Return a result
lessons.summary: `return` sends a value back to the function's caller.

teaching.text: Use `return` when the caller should receive and reuse a computed value.

def double(number):
    return number * 2

result = double(4)
print(result)
# → 8
A returned value is not printed unless you print it.

lessons.task: Define `square(number)` to return the number times itself, then print `square(5)`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define square so it returns number squared
# Print square(5)
hints: Pass the function call directly to `print()`.

lessons.title: 35. Return early
lessons.summary: A `return` statement ends the function immediately.

teaching.text: Use an early return to handle a condition before the rest of the function runs.
Statements after an executed `return` are skipped.

lessons.task: Define `check(number)` to return `zero` when the number is `0`, otherwise return `nonzero`. Print `check(0)`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define check with an early return for zero
# Print check(0)
hints: Return `zero` inside the `if`, then return `nonzero` afterward.

lessons.title: 36. Set a default value
lessons.summary: A default parameter supplies a value when an argument is omitted.

teaching.text: Assign a value in the parameter list to make that argument optional.

def greet(name='friend'):
    print(f'Hello, {name}')

greet()
# → Hello, friend
Passing an argument overrides the default.

lessons.task: Define `set_mode(mode='safe')` to print `Mode: <mode>`, then call it without an argument.
lessons.starterCode: # Define set_mode with safe as the default, then call it

lessons.title: 37. Name arguments explicitly
lessons.summary: Keyword arguments match values to parameters by name.

teaching.text: Pass `parameter=value` to make a function call clearer or change argument order.

def describe(name, level):
    print(f'{name}: {level}')

describe(level=2, name='Ada')
# → Ada: 2
Keyword names must match the function's parameter names.

lessons.task: Define `connect(host, port)` to print `<host>:<port>`, then call it with keyword arguments `port=8000` and `host='localhost'`.
lessons.starterCode: # Define connect, then call it using named arguments

lessons.title: 38. Keep values local
lessons.summary: Variables created inside a function belong to that function.

teaching.text: A local variable exists while its function runs and is accessed inside that function.

def build_message():
    message = 'Ready'
    print(message)

build_message()
# → Ready
Trying to use `message` outside the function would raise an error.

lessons.task: Define `report()` with a local variable `status = 'Complete'`, print it inside the function, then call `report()`.
lessons.starterCode: # Define report with a local status variable, then call it
hints: Create `status` inside the indented function body.
Print it before the function ends.

lessons.title: 39. Read an outer value
lessons.summary: A function can read a variable defined in an enclosing outer scope.

teaching.text: When a name is not local, Python looks outward for a matching variable.

prefix = 'ID'

def show(value):
    print(f'{prefix}-{value}')

show(7)
# → ID-7
Prefer parameters when a function should explicitly depend on a value.

lessons.task: Create `app = 'seec0de'`. Define `show_app()` to print that outer variable, then call it.
lessons.starterCode:
# Create app outside the function
# Define show_app to print it, then call the function

lessons.title: 40. Combine functions
lessons.summary: One function can call another and use its returned value.

teaching.text: Small functions become more useful when their outputs feed other functions.
The inner function runs first.

lessons.task: Define `triple(number)` and `add_two(number)`, then print the result of adding two to triple of `4`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define triple and add_two
# Combine them so the output is 14

tracks.name: Python — Next Level
tracks.description: Model objects, handle failures, and write compact transformations.

lessons.title: 41. Define a class
lessons.summary: A class defines a reusable type of object.

teaching.text: Use `class` to group data and behavior under a new type name.

class Robot:
    pass

bot = Robot()
print(type(bot).**name**)
# → Robot
`pass` creates an empty body while keeping the class valid.

lessons.task: Define an empty class named `Lesson`, create one object, and print its type name.
lessons.starterCode: # Define Lesson, create an object, and print its type name
hints: Use `class Lesson:` followed by an indented `pass`.
lessons.solution:
class Lesson:
    pass

lesson = Lesson()
print(type(lesson).**name**)

lessons.title: 42. Initialize an object
lessons.summary: The `__init__()` method sets up a new object's initial state.

teaching.text: `__init__()` runs when an object is created. Its first parameter, `self`, refers to that object.
Instance attributes are commonly created with `self.attribute = value`.

lessons.task: Define `Course` so its initializer stores a `title`. Create `Course('Python')` and print its title.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define Course with an initializer that stores title
# Create a Python course and print its title

lessons.title: 43. Give objects their own data
lessons.summary: Each object can store its own attribute values.

teaching.text: Objects created from the same class can hold different values in the same attributes.

class Player:
    def **init**(self, score):
        self.score = score

first = Player(10)
second = Player(20)
print(first.score)
print(second.score)
# → 10
# → 20
Changing one object's attribute does not change another object's attribute.

lessons.task: Define `Device` with a `name` attribute. Create devices named `Laptop` and `Tablet`, then print both names.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define Device with a name attribute
# Create Laptop and Tablet devices, then print both names
lessons.solution:
class Device:
    def **init**(self, name):
        self.name = name

first = Device('Laptop')
second = Device('Tablet')
print(first.name)
print(second.name)

lessons.title: 44. Add object behavior
lessons.summary: An instance method defines behavior that uses an object's data.

teaching.text: Define a method inside a class and include `self` as its first parameter.
Call an instance method with `object.method()`.

lessons.task: Define `Button` with a method `click()` that prints `Clicked`, then create a button and call the method.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define Button.click so it prints Clicked
# Create a button and call click

lessons.title: 45. Update object state
lessons.summary: A method can read and change an object's attributes.

teaching.text: Use `self` inside a method to update the current object's state.
The updated attribute remains on the object after the method finishes.

lessons.task: Define `Counter` starting at `0`, add an `increment()` method, call it twice, and print the value.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define Counter with value 0 and an increment method
# Increment twice, then print the value

lessons.title: 46. Catch an exception
lessons.summary: A `try`/`except` block handles an error without crashing the program.

teaching.text: Put risky code in `try` and error-handling code in `except`.
Catch specific exception types instead of every possible error.

lessons.task: Try to convert `hello` to an integer. Catch `ValueError` and print `Invalid number`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Try int('hello')
# Catch ValueError and print Invalid number

lessons.title: 47. Handle success after try
lessons.summary: An `else` block runs when the `try` block finishes without an exception.

teaching.text: Use `else` for work that should happen only after the risky code succeeds.
Keeping success logic in `else` makes the `try` block smaller.

lessons.task: Convert `42` from a string to an integer in `try`, catch `ValueError`, and print the value in `else`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Convert '42' inside try
# Catch ValueError and print the value in else
hints: Add `else:` after the `except` block and print `value`.

lessons.title: 48. Raise an exception
lessons.summary: Use `raise` to signal that a value or state is invalid.

teaching.text: A function can raise a specific exception when its input breaks a rule.
Raising an exception stops normal execution until it is handled.

lessons.task: Define `check_score(score)` to raise `ValueError` when the score is below `0`. Call it with `-5`, catch the error, and print `Invalid score`.
lessons.starterCode:
# Define check_score and raise ValueError for a negative score
# Call it with -5 and handle the error

lessons.title: 49. Build a list compactly
lessons.summary: A list comprehension creates a list by transforming each item from an iterable.

teaching.text: Write the output expression first, followed by a `for` clause inside square brackets.

squares = [number * number for number in range(1, 4)]
print(squares)
# → [1, 4, 9]
Use a normal loop when the transformation needs several steps.

lessons.task: Use a list comprehension to double the values in `[1, 2, 3]`, then print the new list.
lessons.starterCode:
# Double each value in [1, 2, 3] with a list comprehension
# Print the result

lessons.title: 50. Filter a comprehension
lessons.summary: Add an `if` clause to keep only items that match a condition.

teaching.text: Place an `if` condition after the `for` clause to filter the source values.

evens = [number for number in range(1, 7) if number % 2 == 0]
print(evens)
# → [2, 4, 6]
The condition decides which source items enter the new list.

lessons.task: Use a list comprehension to keep only numbers greater than `3` from `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`, then print the result.
lessons.starterCode:
# Keep values greater than 3 with a list comprehension
# Print the result
lessons.solution:
filtered = [number for number in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] if number > 3]
print(filtered)
